title: Die Hochzeit der Frau Füchsin
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 38. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 38 in the Grimms’ collection, printed as two short tales on a single theme: how Mrs. Fox is courted after the death of her husband, the old fox with nine tails. In the first, the old fox only feigns death to test his wife’s fidelity, then springs up and thrashes the wedding party; in the second he is truly dead, and a parade of suitors — wolf, dog, stag, hare, bear, lion — is turned away until a young fox matches his red legs and pointed snout. The cat-maid receives each suitor at the door in rhyming dialogue, and the counting of tails and markings decides who may wed the widow. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, ward, schloß, daß, mußte, todt, Noth, Thüre, Thor, Hausthüre, Wittwe, rothe, Waldthiere and mausetodt, alongside dialect rhyme-words like gan, hinan and rummer.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein alter Fuchs mit neun Schwänzen, der glaubte seine Frau wäre ihm nicht treu und wollte er sie in Versuchung führen.
sentences.l: There was once an old fox with nine tails, who believed his wife were not faithful to him and wanted he to lead her into temptation.
sentences.i: There was once an old fox with nine tails, who thought his wife was not faithful to him and wanted to lead her into temptation.
sentences.n: This is the first of two little tales printed together under one title — both tell how Mrs. Fox is courted after her husband’s death (feigned in this first tale, real in the second). “wäre” is the reported-speech subjunctive (he believed she was unfaithful). “und wollte er” fronts the verb before the subject, a loosely joined older construction.

sentences.t: Er streckte sich unter die Bank, regte kein Glied und stellte sich als wenn er mausetodt wäre.
sentences.l: He stretched himself under the bench, stirred no limb, and made himself as if he were stone-dead.
sentences.i: He stretched himself out under the bench, stirred not a limb, and pretended to be stone-dead.
sentences.n: “sich stellen als wenn …” = to pretend, act as if (modern als ob). “mausetodt” (modern mausetot), literally ‘mouse-dead,’ is an intensive: stone-dead, dead as a doornail. The fox feigns death to test his wife’s fidelity — the test-of-faithfulness motif that drives the tale.

sentences.t: Die Frau Füchsin gieng auf ihre Kammer, schloß sich ein, und ihre Magd, die Jungfer Katze, saß auf dem Herd und kochte.
sentences.l: Mrs. Fox went up to her chamber, shut herself in, and her maid, the maiden Cat, sat on the hearth and cooked.
sentences.i: Mrs. Fox went up to her chamber and shut herself in, while her maid, Miss Cat, sat by the hearth and cooked.
sentences.n: “Frau Füchsin” is the she-fox, Mrs. Fox (Füchsin is the feminine of Fuchs). Her maid is a cat — “Jungfer Katze,” Miss (Maiden) Cat, addressed later as “mein Kätzchen.” “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (went); “schloß” of schloss. “sich einschließen” (to shut oneself in) is separable.

sentences.t: Als es nun bekannt ward, daß der alte Fuchs gestorben war, so meldeten sich die Freier.
sentences.l: When it now became known that the old fox had died, then the suitors announced themselves.
sentences.i: Now when it became known that the old fox had died, the suitors came forward.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (became); “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass. “sich melden” = to present oneself, come forward. “die Freier” = the suitors, a whole line of whom will call at the door.

sentences.t: Da hörte die Magd daß jemand vor der Hausthüre stand und anklopfte; sie gieng und machte auf, und da wars ein junger Fuchs, der sprach
sentences.l: Then the maid heard that someone stood before the house-door and knocked; she went and opened up, and there it was a young fox, who spoke
sentences.i: Then the maid heard someone standing at the front door and knocking; she went and opened it, and there stood a young fox, who said:
sentences.n: “Hausthüre” = modern Haustür (front door), with the old -th-. “machte auf” (aufmachen, to open) is separable. “wars” = war es (it was), contracted. The sentence breaks off; the door-verse follows.

sentences.t:
„Was macht sie, Jungfer Katze?
schläft se oder wacht se?“
sentences.l:
“What is she doing, Miss Cat?
sleeps she or wakes she?”
sentences.i:
“What are you about, Miss Cat —
are you asleep, or awake?”
sentences.n: The suitor addresses the maid in the polite third person: “Was macht sie?” = ‘what are you doing?’ “se” is a clipped colloquial ‘sie.’ “wacht” (from wachen) = is awake, watching. The cat answers in the next verse.

sentences.t: Sie antwortete
sentences.l: She answered

sentences.t:
„ich schlafe nicht, ich wache.
Will er wissen was ich mache?
Ich koche warm Bier, thue Butter hinein:
will der Herr mein Gast sein?“
sentences.l:
“I sleep not, I wake.
Does he want to know what I do?
I cook warm beer, put butter in:
will the gentleman be my guest?”
sentences.i:
“I am not sleeping, I am awake.
Would you like to know what I am doing?
I am warming beer and putting butter in it —
will you be my guest, sir?”
sentences.n: The cat answers with the same polite third person (“Will er wissen…?” = ‘would you like to know?’; “der Herr” = ‘you, sir’). “thue” is the 1857 spelling of tue (thun = tun); “Butter hinein thun” = to put butter in. Warm beer with butter was a real old household drink.

sentences.t: „Ich bedanke mich, Jungfer,“ sagte der Fuchs, „was macht die Frau Füchsin?“
sentences.l: “I thank you, maiden,” said the fox, “what is Mrs. Fox doing?”
sentences.n: “sich bedanken” = to give thanks — here to decline the offered food politely before asking after the widow.

sentences.t: Die Magd antwortete
sentences.l: The maid answered

sentences.t:
„sie sitzt auf ihrer Kammer,
sie beklagt ihren Jammer,
weint ihre Äuglein seidenroth,
weil der alte Herr Fuchs ist todt.“
sentences.l:
“she sits in her chamber,
she bewails her woe,
weeps her little-eyes silk-red,
because old Master Fox is dead.”
sentences.i:
“she sits up in her chamber,
lamenting her sorrow,
weeping her little eyes silk-red,
for old Master Fox is dead.”
sentences.n: “Äuglein” = little eyes (diminutive of Auge). “seidenroth” (silk-red) = red as dyed silk, i.e. weeping her eyes red. “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot (dead). Note the rhymes Kammer/Jammer and seidenroth/todt.

sentences.t: „Sag sie ihr doch, Jungfer, es wäre ein junger Fuchs da, der wollte sie gerne freien.“ „Schon gut, junger Herr.“
sentences.l: “Do tell her, maiden, there were a young fox here who would gladly court her.” “Very well, young sir.”
sentences.i: “Do tell her, miss, that a young fox is here who would gladly court her.” “Very well, young sir.”
sentences.n: “Sag sie” is again the polite third-person address to the cat (‘tell her, will you’). “wäre … wollte” are reported-speech subjunctive (what he asks her to relay). “freien” = to woo, court, seek in marriage.

sentences.t:
Da gieng die Katz die Tripp die Trapp,
Da schlug die Thür die Klipp die Klapp.
„Frau Füchsin, sind Sie da?“
„Ach ja, mein Kätzchen, ja.“
„Es ist ein Freier draus.“
„Mein Kind, wie sieht er aus?“
sentences.l:
Then went the cat the trip the trap,
then struck the door the click the clack.
“Mrs. Fox, are you there?”
“Oh yes, my little-cat, yes.”
“There is a suitor outside.”
“My child, how does he look?”
sentences.i:
Then the cat went trip-trap,
then the door went click-clack.
“Mrs. Fox, are you there?”
“Oh yes, my kitten, yes.”
“There’s a suitor outside.”
“My child, what does he look like?”
sentences.n: “Tripp/Trapp” and “Klipp/Klapp” are sound-words for the cat’s pattering feet and the banging door. Here Mrs. Fox switches to the formal “Sie” (“sind Sie da”). “draus” = da draußen (outside). “sieht … aus” (aussehen, to look, appear) is separable.

sentences.t: „Hat er denn auch neun so schöne Zeiselschwänze wie der selige Herr Fuchs?“
sentences.l: “Has he then also nine such fine siskin-tails like the late Master Fox?”
sentences.i: “But has he nine fine bushy tails like my late Master Fox?”
sentences.n: Here the suitor test is by tails: the widow will take only a fox with as many tails as her husband’s nine. “Zeiselschwänze”: Zeisel = siskin (a small finch); the compound suggests fine, feathery, bushy tails. “selig” = late, deceased (‘blessed’).

sentences.t: „Ach nein,“ antwortete die Katze, „er hat nur Einen.“
sentences.l: “Oh no,” answered the cat, “he has only one.”
sentences.n: “Einen” is capitalized to stress the count — only a single tail, so the suitor fails the test.

sentences.t: „So will ich ihn nicht haben.“
sentences.l: “Then I will not have him.”

sentences.t: Die Jungfer Katze gieng hinab und schickte den Freier fort.
sentences.l: Miss Cat went down and sent the suitor away.
sentences.n: “gieng hinab” (hinabgehen, to go down) and “schickte … fort” (fortschicken, to send away) are separable.

sentences.t: Bald darauf klopfte es wieder an, und war ein anderer Fuchs vor der Thüre, der wollte die Frau Füchsin freien; er hatte zwei Schwänze; aber es gieng ihm nicht besser als dem ersten.
sentences.l: Soon thereafter it knocked again, and there was another fox before the door, who wanted to court Mrs. Fox; he had two tails; but it went for him no better than for the first.
sentences.i: Soon after there was another knock, and another fox stood at the door, who wanted to court Mrs. Fox; he had two tails; but he fared no better than the first.
sentences.n: “klopfte … an” (anklopfen, to knock) is separable. “es gieng ihm nicht besser” uses the impersonal “es geht jemandem” (it goes for someone) — ‘he fared no better.’ “dem ersten” is dative (than the first).

sentences.t: Danach kamen noch andere immer mit einem Schwanz mehr, die alle abgewiesen wurden, bis zuletzt einer kam der neun Schwänze hatte wie der alte Herr Fuchs.
sentences.l: After that came still others, always with one tail more, who were all turned away, until at last one came who had nine tails like the old Master Fox.
sentences.i: After that came still more, each with one tail more than the last, all turned away, until at last one came who had nine tails like old Master Fox.
sentences.n: The tail-counting test in motion: each fox has one tail more than the last, but only the one matching the husband’s nine is accepted. “abgewiesen wurden” is passive, ‘were turned away.’

sentences.t: Als die Wittwe das hörte, sprach sie voll Freude zu der Katze
sentences.l: When the widow heard that, she spoke full of joy to the cat
sentences.i: When the widow heard that, she said joyfully to the cat:
sentences.n: “Wittwe” is the 1857 spelling of Witwe (widow).

sentences.t:
„nun macht mir Thor und Thüre auf,
und kehrt den alten Herrn Fuchs hinaus.“
sentences.l:
“now open me gate and door,
and sweep the old Master Fox out.”
sentences.i:
“now open gate and door for me,
and sweep old Master Fox out of the house.”
sentences.n: “macht … auf” (aufmachen) and “kehrt … hinaus” (hinauskehren, to sweep out) are separable. “Thor” = modern Tor (gate), old -th-. “mir” is a dative of interest (‘for me’). Believing him truly dead, she orders the body swept out.

sentences.t: Als aber eben die Hochzeit sollte gefeiert werden, da regte sich der alte Herr Fuchs unter der Bank, prügelte das ganze Gesindel durch und jagte es mit der Frau Füchsin zum Haus hinaus.
sentences.l: But just when the wedding was to be celebrated, then the old Master Fox stirred under the bench, thrashed the whole rabble through, and chased it, along with Mrs. Fox, out of the house.
sentences.i: But just as the wedding was about to be celebrated, old Master Fox stirred under the bench, gave the whole rabble a sound thrashing, and drove them — Mrs. Fox with them — out of the house.
sentences.n: The fidelity-test pays off: not dead at all, he leaps up. “prügelte … durch” (durchprügeln, to thrash soundly) is separable. “Gesindel” = rabble, riff-raff. “zum Haus hinaus jagen” = to chase out of the house. So ends the first tale.

sentences.t: Als der alte Herr Fuchs gestorben war, kam der Wolf als Freier, klopfte an die Thüre, und die Katze, die als Magd bei der Frau Füchsin diente, machte auf.
sentences.l: When the old Master Fox had died, the wolf came as a suitor, knocked at the door, and the cat, who served as maid with Mrs. Fox, opened up.
sentences.i: When old Master Fox had died, the wolf came courting, knocked at the door, and the cat, who served as maid to Mrs. Fox, opened it.
sentences.n: Here the second little tale begins: the same theme, but now the old fox is really dead, and the suitors are other beasts — first the wolf. “diente” = served (dienen, to serve). “klopfte an” (anklopfen) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Wolf grüßte sie, und sprach
sentences.l: The wolf greeted her, and spoke

sentences.t:
„guten Tag, Frau Katz von Kehrewitz,
wie kommts daß sie alleine sitzt?
was macht sie gutes da?“
sentences.l:
“good day, Mrs. Cat of Kehrewitz,
how comes it that she sits alone?
what good is she doing there?”
sentences.i:
“good day, Mrs. Cat of Kehrewitz,
how is it you sit here alone?
what good are you up to there?”
sentences.n: A mock-grand address: “Frau Katz von Kehrewitz” gives the cat a noble-sounding surname, punning on kehren (to sweep) — a maid’s work. Again the polite third-person “sie” = ‘you.’ “kommts” = kommt es; “alleine” = allein (alone).

sentences.t: Die Katze antwortete
sentences.l: The cat answered

sentences.t:
„Brock mir Wecke und Milch ein:
will der Herr mein Gast sein?“
sentences.l:
“Break me rolls and milk in:
will the gentleman be my guest?”
sentences.i:
“Crumble me bread and milk together —
will you be my guest, sir?”
sentences.n: “einbrocken” (to crumble bread into a liquid) is separable — here “Brock … ein.” “Wecke” = white rolls (Weck). The cat offers the same guest-welcome she gave in the first tale.

sentences.t: „Dank schön, Frau Katze,“ antwortete der Wolf, „die Frau Füchsin nicht zu Haus?“
sentences.l: “Many thanks, Mrs. Cat,” answered the wolf, “Mrs. Fox not at home?”
sentences.i: “Thank you kindly, Mrs. Cat,” answered the wolf; “is Mrs. Fox not at home?”
sentences.n: “die Frau Füchsin nicht zu Haus?” drops the verb (ist … nicht zu Haus?) — a clipped colloquial question. “zu Haus” = at home.

sentences.t: Die Katze sprach
sentences.l: The cat spoke

sentences.t:
„sie sitzt droben in der Kammer,
beweint ihren Jammer,
beweint ihre große Noth,
daß der alte Herr Fuchs ist todt.“
sentences.l:
“she sits up above in the chamber,
bewails her woe,
bewails her great distress,
that old Master Fox is dead.”
sentences.i:
“she sits upstairs in her chamber,
weeping over her sorrow,
weeping over her great distress,
that old Master Fox is dead.”
sentences.n: A varied echo of the first tale’s lament. “droben” = up there, upstairs (da oben). “beweinen” = to weep over, mourn (transitive). “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (need, distress).

sentences.t: Der Wolf antwortete
sentences.l: The wolf answered

sentences.t:
„Will sie haben einen andern Mann,
so soll sie nur herunter gan.“
Die Katz die lief die Trepp hinan,
und ließ ihr Zeilchen rummer gan
bis sie kam vor den langen Saal:
klopft an mit ihren fünf goldenen Ringen.
„Frau Füchsin, ist sie drinnen?
Will sie haben einen andern Mann,
so soll sie nur herunter gan.“
sentences.l:
“If she wants to have another husband,
then let her just come down.”
The cat, she ran up the stairs,
and let her little-tail run round,
until she came before the long hall:
knocks with her five golden rings.
“Mrs. Fox, is she inside?
If she wants to have another husband,
then let her just come down.”
sentences.i:
“If she would have another husband,
then let her only come down.”
The cat ran up the stairs,
and let her little tail swing along,
till she came to the long hall:
she knocks with her five golden rings.
“Mrs. Fox, are you within?
If you would have another husband,
then only come down.”
sentences.n: Dialect rhyme-forms fill this verse: “gan” = gehen (to go), “hinan” = up, “rummer” ≈ herum (round about), “Zeilchen” a little tail (dialect diminutive, cf. Zagel), “klopft” = klopft an (knocks). “Die Katz die lief” doubles the subject with a resuming “die,” a folk-song rhythm. The cat wears five golden rings on her paw.

sentences.t: Die Frau Füchsin fragte „hat der Herr rothe Höslein an, und hat er ein spitz Mäulchen?“
sentences.l: Mrs. Fox asked, “does the gentleman have red little-breeches on, and has he a pointed little-snout?”
sentences.i: Mrs. Fox asked, “does the gentleman wear little red breeches, and has he a pointed little snout?”
sentences.n: Now the suitor test is by looks: the new husband must match the fox’s markings — reddish hind legs (“rothe Höslein,” the fox’s red fur like little breeches) and a pointed muzzle (“spitz Mäulchen”). “hat … an” (anhaben, to have on, wear) is separable. “rothe” is the 1857 spelling of rote.

sentences.t: „Nein“ antwortete die Katze.
sentences.l: “No,” answered the cat.

sentences.t: „So kann er mir nicht dienen.“
sentences.l: “Then he cannot serve me.”
sentences.i: “Then he will not do for me.”
sentences.n: “er kann mir nicht dienen” = he is of no use to me, he won’t do (again the polite “er” = ‘he/you’). Lacking the fox’s red legs and pointed snout, the wolf is refused.

sentences.t: Als der Wolf abgewiesen war, kam ein Hund, ein Hirsch, ein Hase, ein Bär, ein Löwe, und nach einander alle Waldthiere.
sentences.l: When the wolf had been turned away, there came a dog, a stag, a hare, a bear, a lion, and one after another all the forest-animals.
sentences.i: When the wolf had been turned away, there came a dog, a stag, a hare, a bear, a lion, and one after another all the beasts of the forest.
sentences.n: “Hirsch” = stag; “Hase” = hare; “Bär” = bear; “Löwe” = lion. “Waldthiere” = modern Waldtiere (forest animals), with the old -th-. Each will fail the same test.

sentences.t: Aber es fehlte immer eine von den guten Eigenschaften, die der alte Herr Fuchs gehabt hatte, und die Katze mußte den Freier jedesmal wegschicken.
sentences.l: But there was always lacking one of the good qualities that the old Master Fox had had, and the cat had to send the suitor away each time.
sentences.i: But one of the good qualities that old Master Fox had had was always missing, and the cat had to send the suitor away every time.
sentences.n: “fehlen” = to be lacking, missing. “Eigenschaften” = qualities, traits. “wegschicken” (to send away) is separable. “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste.

sentences.t: Endlich kam ein junger Fuchs.
sentences.l: At last came a young fox.
sentences.i: At last a young fox came.

sentences.t: Da sprach die Frau Füchsin „hat der Herr rothe Höslein an, und hat er ein spitz Mäulchen?“
sentences.l: Then Mrs. Fox spoke, “does the gentleman have red little-breeches on, and has he a pointed little-snout?”
sentences.i: Then Mrs. Fox said, “does the gentleman wear little red breeches, and has he a pointed little snout?”
sentences.n: The same test repeated — now for a young fox, who alone can pass it.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte die Katze, „das hat er.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the cat, “that he has.”

sentences.t: „So soll er herauf kommen“ sprach die Frau Füchsin, und hieß die Magd das Hochzeitfest bereiten.
sentences.l: “Then he shall come up,” spoke Mrs. Fox, and bade the maid prepare the wedding-feast.
sentences.i: “Then let him come up,” said Mrs. Fox, and bade the maid make ready the wedding feast.
sentences.n: “herauf kommen” (heraufkommen, to come up) is separable. “heißen” here = to bid, order (jemanden etwas heißen). “Hochzeitfest” = wedding feast.

sentences.t:
„Katze, kehr die Stube aus,
und schmeiß den alten Fuchs zum Fenster hinaus.
Bracht so manche dicke fette Maus,
fraß sie immer alleine,
gab mir aber keine.“
sentences.l:
“Cat, sweep the room out,
and fling the old fox out of the window.
[He] brought so many a thick fat mouse,
ate them always alone,
but gave me none.”
sentences.i:
“Cat, sweep out the parlour,
and fling the old fox out of the window.
Many a plump, fat mouse he brought home,
but always ate them by himself
and never gave me one.”
sentences.n: Mrs. Fox’s little song of grievance against her dead husband: “kehr … aus” (auskehren, to sweep out) and “schmeiß … hinaus” (hinausschmeißen, to fling out) are separable. “Bracht” = brachte (brought), with ‘he’ understood. “fraß” (fressen) is the verb for an animal devouring. The old fox is discarded without ceremony.

sentences.t: Da ward die Hochzeit gehalten mit dem jungen Herrn Fuchs, und ward gejubelt und getanzt, und wenn sie nicht aufgehört haben, so tanzen sie noch.
sentences.l: Then the wedding was held with the young Master Fox, and there was rejoicing and dancing, and if they have not stopped, then they are still dancing.
sentences.i: Then the wedding was held with the young Master Fox, and there was rejoicing and dancing; and if they have not stopped, they are dancing still.
sentences.n: “ward … gehalten / ward gejubelt und getanzt” is the archaic passive with ward, here impersonal (‘there was rejoicing and dancing’). “aufgehört” (aufhören, to stop) is separable. The closing “und wenn sie nicht aufgehört haben, so tanzen sie noch” is a traditional fairy-tale ending formula.
